kept.text:
Big engines, bigger numbers… and yes, you’re still expected to remember them 😅✈️

1. GE90 Series (General Electric)
Models: GE90-76B, -85B, -94B, -110B1, -115B
Thrust: 76,000 to 127,900 lbf

The GE90-115B? Still the most powerful commercial jet engine out there 💪
Found on: 777-200LR, 777-300ER, 777F

2. Trent 800 Series (Rolls-Royce)
Models: Trent 875, 877, 884, 892, 895
Thrust: 75,000 to 95,000 lbf

Classic choice for the 777-200 and 777-300

3. PW4000 Series (Pratt & Whitney)
Models: PW4074, 4077, 4084, 4090, 4098
Thrust: 74,000 to 98,000 lbf

Also powering the 777-200 and 777-300

Same aircraft… three different ways to get you off the ground 🚀

With @AirheadAtpl

kept.text:
$GE just landed a $107.8M defense order for J85 aircraft engines — funded by FY2025 budget with a 5.5-year base contract extendable to 10 years. That's sticky, recurring revenue baked in.

Defense backlog keeps building for $GE Aerospace. Long-duration contracts like this reduce revenue uncertainty and support margin visibility — exactly what institutional buyers want to see.

Watching $GE for continued strength on defense catalyst flow. Any dip is a buy-the-news setup imo.

#GE #DefenseStocks #Aerospace

kept.text:
A former $GE CEO sent me a DM saying I was dead right about the split.

He told me he had suggested the same idea to the board years earlier and was effectively laughed out of the room.

The objection?

“You can’t destroy a 130-year legacy.”

That is exactly why these opportunities exist.

Boards often confuse legacy with structure. They protect the company they remember, not the company the market is actually valuing.

GE did not destroy its legacy by splitting up.

It finally unlocked it.

profile_bio.description: Most investors follow markets. I follow mispriced change. Spinoffs • restructurings • special situations. Founder, The Edge.
